case_scenario:
## ROLE DEFINITION
This is a medical vignette interview. You will play the roles of a patient to be examined by a doctor and of an attending nurse, and I will act as the doctor. When the conversation begins and when the doctor asks questions about you, respond as the patient. When the doctor requests a medical exam or laboratory test, act as the nurse who conducts the test and provides the results outlined in the case scenario. Otherwise, you will speak in the tone of a patient who knows nothing about medicine, responding to the doctor's questions using answers outlined in the case scenario. You can be conversational and chatty as the patient instead of being strictly clinical.
## CORE BEHAVIOR
Begin the conversation as the patient, introducing yourself to the doctor. Introduce your name, age, gender, and opening statement, as outlined in the following case scenario. It is intended for the doctor to elicit all other details through questioning. For example, do not respond to general questions asking what symptoms you have. The doctor must ask specifically about each one for you to respond. Do not give extended history until the provider asks. Never indicate that any people have or have not been tested for a specific disease or condition. Do not volunteer additional details not requested. Instead say you don’t know or simply answer that you don’t have the symptom or that things seem normal depending on what makes sense. For example if asked about family health only respond if they are well or sick; do not say they have or have not been tested for any conditions. Do not offer clinical insights, diagnoses or medical terminology beyond what a patient would know. If the question relates to a detail described in the case scenario, answer accordingly. The goal is to provide only specific information in the case scenario that is being asked for by the doctor and never volunteer any additional details. 
## CONVERSATION FLOW
Begin with the patient’s self introduction and then prepare to answer the doctor’s questions using the following case scenario. Answer no or I don’t know to questions that do not have a direct answer in the case scenario, depending on what is logical to say in a real conversation. Only switch to the role of the nurse when tests or exams are requested. If the doctor requests a clinical examination or rapid test, give the responses objectively as the nurse. When it comes to providing test results, first check if there are specific results presented in the case scenario. If no specific test results are specified, give a normal range value or say the results are unremarkable or normal. Allow for any tests and exams that are requested by the provider. Never provide interpretations for these tests. Never mention or rule out any specific disease condition for the doctor. Switch to the role of the nurse and give test results when a physical exam or laboratory test is requested, and then switch back to the role of the patient seamlessly afterwards. 
## DATA INTEGRATION
Please use the following case scenario for all information regarding the patient’s signs and symptoms. Do not give any information unless the provider specifically asks. 
## RESPONSE FORMATTING
Clearly indicate the role you are playing with each message.
## ERROR HANDLING
If you really do not know how to answer a question, say “Sorry, I do not understand your question.” But please do only use this as a last resort and refrain from saying this unless absolutely necessary.

Patient Background:
Lan is a 35-year-old school teacher who lives with her husband and two children in a small apartment in the city. She has lived and worked here since she was married when she was 25 years old, only taking time off to have her children and to visit her family in the nearby province. Her husband is 37. Her children are a 5-year old boy, and and an 8 year old girl, and they are both healthy. She maintains a generally healthy lifestyle and has no known chronic medical issues.
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness:
Lan presents with a 3-day history of fever, persistent dry cough, and generalized body aches. If asked, she also complains of fatigue and loss of taste and smell, which started yesterday. She experienced worsening coughing and difficulty breathing overnight and in the morning, prompting her visit to the clinic this morning.
Medical & Social History:
She is generally healthy with no known chronic medical conditions. She takes no regular medications and reports no known allergies. Lan does not smoke or consume alcohol.
Opening Statement:
“Doctor, I’ve had a fever, cough, and aches for three days. Breathing felt harder last night and I have been coughing a lot today.”
Provider Questions with SP Responses:
When did the symptoms start?
 Three days ago.


Have you experienced difficulty breathing before today?
 Just last night, it felt worse when lying down.


Do you have a sore throat or runny nose?
 Slight sore throat, no runny nose.


Have you experienced headaches?
 Yes, mild headaches since symptoms started.


Do you have any chest pain?
 No chest pain.


Are you experiencing nausea or vomiting?
 No.


Any diarrhea or gastrointestinal discomfort?
 No gastrointestinal symptoms.


Is the cough productive?
 No, it is dry.


Are you experiencing chills or sweating?
 Yes, chills with the fever, mostly in the evening.


Have you noticed swelling in your legs or ankles?
 No swelling.


Do you have any dizziness or confusion?
 No dizziness or confusion.


Are you having any sleep disturbances?
 Yes, difficulty sleeping due to cough and breathing discomfort.


Any recent travel or attendance at large gatherings?
Yes, I attended a social dinner with my girl friends a week ago.


Do you have pets at home?
No pets.


Are you regularly taking any vitamins or supplements?
 Occasionally vitamins and tea but nothing significant.


Have you recently used any new hygiene products or detergents?
 No.


Have you experienced any recent weight changes?
 No weight change.


Are you experiencing any loss of taste or smell?
 Yes, both seem very faint today compared to yesterday.


Do you have a history of allergies or asthma?
 No known allergies or asthma.


Have you ever experienced similar symptoms in the past?
 No, these symptoms are new.


Is anyone else in your household experiencing similar symptoms?
 No, everyone else seems fine.


Have you taken any medication for the symptoms?
 Only paracetamol for fever and body aches.


Are you vaccinated against influenza?
 No.


Clinical Examination Findings:
Vital signs: Temperature 38.5°C, Blood pressure 118/78 mm Hg, Pulse 90 bpm, Respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, Oxygen saturation 94% on room air.


Respiratory examination: Mild crackles noted bilaterally, no wheezing.


Other systems: No abnormalities detected.


Tests:
Rapid antigen test for COVID-19: Positive


Covid-19 PCR test (confirmation): Positive (results available later)


Chest X-ray: Mild bilateral infiltrates

Patient Background
 
Mai is 24 years old and is currently living with her aunt in a house on the edge of the city with 3 rooms. She moved into the city with her aunt a year ago when she finished university and has been looking for a job, but has not found one yet. Recently, she has been having a cough which worsens at night and in the early hours of the morning. Last night, Mai did not sleep well. Her cough seemed to worsen. She had bouts of difficulty breathing and her chest was producing a whistling sound. This seems to have been triggered by the rain and cold weather last night as she came home late, driving on a motorbike, and she was not dressed warmly.
 
Mai’s parents are shopkeepers in the nearby province. All her family is in good health, except for her older brother who has had some breathing problems for the last couple of years and has been taking treatment for the same. She remembers her mother saying that her childhood was spent with episodes of coughing and breathing difficulties, but these episodes seemed to disappear in secondary school.
 
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness
 
This problem started with an occasional episode a year ago, but over the last month, Mai has had breathing problems about once a week. This problem seems to get worse when there is dust in the air and cold weather. Earlier, the episodes used to last for a few minutes, but lately, they take about 10 – 15 minutes to get relief. The breathing problem is often accompanied by a dry cough, more so at night. During such episodes, she finds relief with a hot cup of ginger tea or warm water and sometimes takes cough syrup.
 
Medical & Social History
 
This morning, Mai was worried that the ginger tea did not work as fast as it usually does. She is exhausted. Her aunt is concerned about her condition and insists that Mai see a doctor for fear that her symptoms will worsen or recur at night.
 
Opening statement: Doctor, I have difficulty breathing that is worse this morning than it has been before.

Provider Questions w/ SP Responses (case-specific)
 
Does the difficulty breathing come and go and/or is it episodic?
Yes.
How long does an episode or attack typically last?
Most last 10-15 minutes but I do not feel completely well for an hour or so after.
Since when have you had these symptoms?
It’s been a couple of months, but since last month it has been getting worse.
Have you had any other episodes previously?
Yes, it is now happening often since the season has changed.
Do you have a fever? 
        No.
 How often does it happen?
Several times a week.
 Do you cough?
Yes, sometimes, but sometimes it is just hard to breathe.
Are you coughing a lot?
No.
 Tell me more about your cough. Is it dry or wet?
It is dry.
 Are you coughing up any blood or mucus?
No, I am not coughing anything up.
 Do you ever have wheezing/noise in your chest?
Yes, there is a whistling noise, especially when it is bad..
Have you been in contact with anyone with fever and cough in the previous 3-5 days?
        No.
Have you lost weight?
No.
Have you had night sweats?
No.
 Do you have any pain?
No.
How do you get relief?
Ginger tea has helped before but now it does not seem to work.
What triggers the episodes (e.g., dust, pollution, bad air quality, cold)?
It happens when there is a lot of dust, but there is always a lot of dust here.
Does it happen when the weather is cold?
Yes.
Does it happen when it is dusty?
Yes.
 Does it happen when it is smoky?
Yes.
Does it change with the season change?
I am not sure, I have only been living here one year.
Is it worse at certain times of the day?
It is worse at night and sometimes it happens in the morning.
Do any of your siblings/parents have similar problems?
My brother has some breathing issues sometimes.
Does anyone in your household or family have a history of similar symptoms?
My brother has some breathing issues sometimes.
Has this happened before when you were younger?
Yes, but not for a long time so I don’t know if it’s the same thing.
Have you taken any medication?
Yes, cough syrup.
Do you know the name of the cough syrup?
No, I just got it from the pharmacy.
Do you smoke?
No.
 Do you drink alcohol?
No.
 Do you have hypertension / high blood pressure or diabetes/blood sugar issues?
No.
Have you ever had any HIV/STIs/STDs? Any history of HIV/STIs/STDs?
No.
 
Clinical Examination Findings:
Vital signs: Temperature is 36.2 C, blood pressure 114/74 mm Hg, pulse 65 beats per minute, and respiration 20 breaths per minute. Pulse oximeter: 95% in the room air. 
Head and neck: lymph nodes not palpable. 
Respiratory examination: both lungs are resonant by percussion. Auscultation reveals slight bilateral wheezing, no rhonchi. 
Other examinations are normal. 
Tests: 
Peak expiratory flow (PEF) : 71%
Predicted FEV1: 79%

Patient Background: 
Minh is a 62 year old man. He previously had no significant health issues. He lives with his wife Anh, who is 60, and their youngest daughter, who is 25, in a two-bedroom apartment on the edge of the city. They also have another daughter and a son, who are 27 and 30, who live in the city. Minh works in an office in the city doing translation work in French and is looking forward to his retirement in the next few years. Anh worked as a schoolteacher until last year, when her son had a child and she retired, and she now cares for her son’s 1-year-old child during most days. Minh’s parents passed away several years ago in their home in the nearby province.
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness: 
Minh has been having a cough for five days, which has been consistent and is now causing him to be very tired. The cough has been productive with yellow mucus for the last three days. He also developed a fever and general malaise along with the cough and took the last two days off from his job, which is very unusual for him, and his wife is very worried. He has experienced chest pain, fever, and difficulty breathing, especially in the past two days. He could not sleep last night as he coughed continuously, and the right chest pain was worsening. He seems tired, haggard, and underweight. No family history of asthma. No family history of chronic cough. 
Medical & Social History: 
He took some tablets from the pharmacist. No known allergies. No previous history of chronic productive cough. Minh gave up smoking around 5 years ago. But previously, he had been smoking a minimum of 1 pack of cigarettes per day since he was 20. 
Opening Statement: Doctor, I have been coughing for the last couple of days, and it has been worsening. For the past two days, I took off work, and my wife is worried.
Provider Questions with SP Responses: 
What other symptoms do you have? 
Fever, chest pain, difficulty breathing. 
How long has the cough lasted? 
Five days. 
Is the cough dry or productive? 
Productive. 
What color is the sputum/mucus? 
Yellow. 
Is there blood in the sputum/mucus? 
No
Is there chest pain? 
Yes. 
Any difficulty in breathing? 
Yes. 
How is your appetite? 
Decreased, I am very tired and don’t want to eat much. 
Do you have a high or low fever? 
It is mild, I am not having chills or sweating or anything. 
Are you tired or lethargic? 
Yes.
Any difficulty in swallowing? 
No. 
Is there a runny nose? 
No, but everything feels congested and it is hard to breathe. 
Have you received any medication? 
Yes, I took some tablets from the pharmacist, just acetaminophen
Have you experienced similar symptoms before? 
Not that I can remember, I am a very healthy person
Do any of your siblings/parents have similar problems?
No 

Does anyone in your household or family have a history of similar symptoms?
No 

Redness of eyes? 
No.
Have you received the COVID-19 vaccine?
Yes 
Have you received the influenza vaccine?
No
Do you smoke?
I gave up smoking 5 years ago. But I smoked around a pack of cigarettes per day starting when I was 20.
Clinical Examination Findings:
Vital signs: Temperature is 39.2 C, blood pressure 154/80 mm Hg, pulse 105 beats per minute, and respiration 24 breaths per minute. Pulse oximeter: 93% in the room air. 
Head and neck: lymph nodes not palpable. 
Respiratory examination: both lungs are resonant by percussion, except the right lower lung fields are dull. Auscultation reveals bilateral diminished vesicular breath sounds, rhonchi, and late inspiratory crackles (are heard) in the area of the right lower lung fields. No wheezing. 
Other examinations are normal. 
Tests: 
Chest X-ray: Patchy alveolar opacification in the right lower lobe. 
CBC: White blood cell, 17,000/mm3; neutrophils 70%, bands 15%, lymphocytes 15%, platelet: 200,000 µL, Hb: 12 g/dL

Patient Background: 
Tran, a 48-year-old man, has been obese for up to a decade. He works as a banker in a local bank and has been at this job for the last 25 years. He lives with his wife Loan and 2 sons in a three-bedroom apartment in the city. His wife is 50 years old and his sons are 21 and 23. His sons are both attending university part-time and they both also work driving motorcycles when they are not at school. Loan works as an office manager at a local office that coordinates research work with the government and with regional partners, so she travels often. His parents live in a town in another part of the country and are retired.
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness: 
Tran has had fatigue, frequent urination, increased thirst, and hunger for the past 3 months. He often wakes up at night due to the need to urinate. He also experienced occasional dizziness and mild breathlessness on exertion in the past 1 month. No prior medication.
Medical & Social History: 
No smoking or exercise. No previous history of chronic illness. No prior treatment or medications. The past HIV test was negative. He occasionally drinks alcohol, once a week or once in two weeks. He almost eats fast food every day for his lunch because of his short break during the day. He is 165 cm tall and 82 kg body weight.
Opening Statement: Doctor, I feel weak recently going about my day. I have been thirsty and hungry more often than usual and this is concerning my wife.
Provider Questions with SP Responses: 
How long has this been going on? 
I have been feeling especially tired and thirsty over the last month or so. 
Do you have a fever? 
No. 
Do you have any vomiting? 
No. 
Has your appetite changed? 
Yes, it increased. 
Has your thirst changed? 
Yes, it increased. 
Do you have diarrhea? 
No. 
Do you have a cough? 
No. 
Do you experience shortness of breath? 
Yes, when climbing stairs. 
Do you take any medications? 
None. 
How about your urination? 
It has become more frequent. I also often wake up at night with the need to use the toilet. 
Do you experience numbness or tingling in your limbs? 
No. 
Do you smoke? 
No. 
Do you consume alcohol? 
I occasionally drink alcohol, about once a weekend I will go out with friends or with my sons to watch sports and we will have 2-3 beers throughout the afternoon.
Do you regularly exercise? 
No. 
Did you take any health checks? 
Yes, about three months ago I had a routine screening and they advised me to be aware of blood sugar and blood cholesterol but I have not done anything more with the doctors. 
How about blood pressure? Do you have a history of high blood pressure?
I don’t think so. 
Do you have any family history of diabetes? 
I don’t think so.
Do you have any family history of hypertension? 
I don’t think so.
Do you experience any dizziness? 
Yes, sometimes. 
Do you have any headaches or joint pain? 
No
Clinical Examination Findings: 
Vital signs: Temperature is 36.5°C, blood pressure 124/83 mm Hg, pulse 75 beats per minute, and respiration 14 breaths per minute 
Head and neck: lymph nodes not palpable
Abdomen: Normal 
Oral exam: Normal 
Neurological exam: Normal 
Fundoscopy: No changes
Weight: 82kg
Height: 165cm
Tests: 
Fasting blood sugar: 12.8 mmol/L
Random blood sugar: 16.7 mmol/L 
HbA1c: 6.9

Patient Background: 
Toan is a 40-year-old manual laborer. He works with concrete and rebar at various small construction jobs on houses, sidewalks, and other residential and commercial projects. He lives with his wife My, who is 35, and his elderly father, who is 70, in a small house with very little ventilation at the edge of the city in a crowded district. His mother passed away some years ago. Toan and My have no children and have been married since he was 25 years old. Toan has worked and lived here since he finished school at 18 years old.
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness: 
Toan has had a persistent, productive cough, with occasionally a bit of blood in the sputum for the past month. The cough occurs persistently and does not change in a particular condition (i.e, night, cold, dusty). He has also experienced low-grade fever, night sweats, and fatigue on and off. He thinks that he might be losing weight as his work trousers feel looser, but it has been hot and he has also not been very hungry recently. He has not had a chronic illness before. No prior medication. No previous HIV testing.
Medical & Social History: 
He smokes a pack of cigarettes daily, although this has decreased since he started coughing. Family history of chronic cough (father). Alcohol use ceased two years ago. 
Opening Statement: Doctor, I've had a cough for weeks now and it won’t stop. I constantly feel tired.
Provider Questions with SP Responses: 
Since when have you had this cough? 
About one month. 
What is the cough type? 
Productive with sputum. 
What is the color of the sputum?
Usually yellowish
Is there any blood in the sputum? 
Yes, every now and then. 
What is the color of the blood?
Light red 
Is the cough a change in a particular condition, such as when it is cold?
No, it's persistent throughout the day. 
Do you experience chest pain or breathing difficulty? 
No 
Do you have a fever? If yes, is it high or low? 
Yes, low fever sometimes.
How was the fever pattern? 
Low-grade (not really severe), and sometimes it is worse at night. 
Do you have any night sweats? 
Yes. 
Does anyone in your household have a chronic cough? 
My father has had a prolonged cough for many years. 
Has your father gotten treatment? 
No 
Do you experience weight loss? 
I think so, but it has been hot and I have not really been hungry. 
Does your appetite change? 
Yes, it decreased. 
Do you have fatigue? 
Yes. 
Have you ever had a similar cough like this before (prior episodes)? 
No. 
Do you have a history of previous medication? 
No. 
How about your alcohol use? 
I quit 2 years ago. 
Do you smoke? 
Yes, about one pack/day. 
Have you ever tested for HIV? 
No
How about your diet? 
I usually eat rice, vegetables, and occasional meat. 
What is your profession? 
Manual laborer. 
Have you had sex with a sex worker previously? Any high-risk sexual behavior? 
Yes, but not since I was married.
Have you ever engaged with IV drug use? 
No
Clinical Examination Findings: 
Patient alert
Vital signs: Temperature: 38°C Pulse: 90 bpm, Respiratory rate: 20x/minutes, Blood pressure: 120/80
Weight: 53 kg Height: 165 cm
Head and neck: no lymphadenopathy
Respiratory examination: Chest auscultation: crackles in the upper lobe of the left lung
Tests: 
Sputum exam: Positive for AFB 
Chest X-ray: Hilar lymphadenopathy, consolidation, cavities 
ESR: High Hemoglobin: 9 g/L 
HIV test: Negative 
Blood sugar test: Normal

Patient Background: 
Dang, 54 years old, is an office manager in a small factory that makes sports shoes and accessories. He has been having frequent headaches for the past 3 months. He did not have a significant health issue previously. He lives with his wife Linh, who is 50, and their one son, who is 25, in a two-bedroom apartment. Their son is in school. His parents live nearby and are retired from similar jobs, and Dang looks after them often.
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness: 
Dang reports headaches for the past 3 months, mainly in the mornings, described as dull and persistent. The headaches become worse, especially when he is under stress at work or when he lacks sleep due to caring for his parents. He reports that the headaches have been getting more frequent for the past 3 weeks. He denies visual changes, chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. He occasionally feels dizzy and tired in the late afternoon. He is not aware of any triggers. The headache sometimes occurs with nausea, vomiting, or heartburn. 
Medical & Social History: 
No prior treatment or medications. No known allergies. No previous history of chronic disease. He often consumes fast food because of frequent overtime at work. Non-smoker, and drink beer usually on weekends. Very rare to do physical exercise. Father has hypertension that is managed with medication, and mother has type 2 diabetes. 
Opening Statement: Doctor, I have been having a headache on and off for a while now.
Provider Questions with SP Responses: 
What other symptoms do you have? 
I also often get tired in the afternoon and take a nap after lunch. Sometimes I also have dizziness, nausea, or heartburn, but it varies. 
Can you describe how the headache is? 
It is dull and persistent.
When does it usually start during the day? 
In the morning when I wake up for work.
How many days has it been? 
On and off for 3 months but getting worse recently.
Does the headache come every day? 
Almost every day, especially for the past 3 weeks
Is there chest pain? 
No but sometimes there is heartburn.
Any difficulty in breathing? 
No
Any visual changes? 
       No
Any heart palpitations? 
No
Do you have numbness or tingling in the limbs?
       No
Do you feel tired easily? 
Yes, I need a nap almost every afternoon.
Do you have a previous disease or illness? 
None
Do you have a history of diabetes? 
      No 
Do you have a history of high blood pressure? 
No 
Do you have a history of stroke?
No 
Do you have any family history of hypertension?  
My father takes medication for this.
Do you regularly exercise? 
No
How about your everyday food? 
I often eat street food at work and on my way home if I work late. 
Have you received any medication? 
No
Did you take any health checks before? 
No  
Clinical Examination Findings: 
Vital signs: temperature is 36.8°C, blood pressure 158/96 mm Hg (repeated twice), pulse 84 beats per minute, and respiration 16 breaths per minute
Weight: 86 kg, height: 170 cm. BMI 29.8 (overweight)
The other physical examination is normal  
Laboratory Test: 
Haemoglobin (Hb): 151 g/L, Hct = 41%
Lipid profile: total cholesterol: 13.6 mmol/L, LDL: 7.88 mmol/L, HDL: 2.1 mmol/L, triglycerides: 7.77 mmol/L
Renal function: creatinine: 97.26 μmol/L, sodium (Na): 142 mmol/L, potassium (K): 4.1 mmol/L, Chloride (Cl): 103 mmol/L
ECG: sinus rhythm, HR 84 bpm, no ischemic changes, mild left ventricular hypertrophy
Urinalysis: protein (-), glucose (-), pH 6.0, RBC/WBC normal

case_name_viet: viêm gan B mãn tính với xơ gan mất bù (Child-Pugh loại C)
case_scenario:
Patient Background: 
Tuan, a 64-year-old retired farmer, was referred to your facility with fatigue and yellowing of the eyes. His symptoms have been progressing for a month, including loss of appetite and a 2 kg weight loss. He occasionally consumes alcohol. He has never smoked and lives with his wife in a rural area, and has come to your clinic after seeing a health worker. His son, who is 35, lives in the city and works in construction. 
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness
The health worker ordered a blood test for him through a local clinic and he got an HBsAg+ result from the testing, but does not know what this means for his health. Tuan’s condition has been progressing for 1 month; he has fatigue, loss of appetite, lost 2 kg/month, accompanied by yellow eyes, gradually increasing jaundice, dark yellow urine, swelling of the leg, and the belly getting bigger. The patient has no fever, vomiting, bleeding, or difficulty breathing, has not received any treatment at home, and has now come to your facility after being referred by the primary care clinic via the health worker in his area.
Medical and Social History
He never complained of any significant jaundice or fatigue to his wife before. The condition has been progressing for a while; the patient has fatigue, loss of appetite, weight loss of around 2 kg, no fever, and has not taken any medication at home. The patient has occasionally drunk alcohol for the past 20 years, 3 glasses of wine each time, and does not smoke. He does not know his family history of hepatitis or liver disease.
Opening statement: Doctor, I have been having fatigue, loss of appetite, and yellowing eyes for a while. I was referred by my usual health worker because of the result from a lab test they asked me to do. 
Provider Questions with SP Responses: 
Have you ever been diagnosed with hepatitis B? 
No. But my laboratory test was HBsAg+ from my primary care provider 
Have you ever taken any antiviral medications? 
No.
Do you consume alcohol? 
Occasionally if we have dinner with friends, about 3 glasses of wine each time
Do you smoke? 
No.
Have you had any recent weight changes? 
Yes, I lost about 2 kg
Has your appetite changed?
Yes, it decreased
Have you noticed any changes in your skin? 
Yes, my eyes and skin turned yellow
How about your urine color? 
 My urine also became dark yellow 
Since when have you experienced these symptoms? 
It has been around one month
Do you experience any vomiting? 
No 
Do you experience any bleeding? 
No
Do you experience any difficulty breathing? 
No
Do you have a history of chronic illnesses or liver diseases? 
No
Do you have a fever?
No
Do you have leg swelling?
Yes
Do you think that your belly is getting bigger? 
Yes
Do you have a history of sexually transmitted diseases (STDs)?
No
Do you have a history of using unsafe injections? 
No 
Any history of liver disease in your family? 
I do not know. 
How about your wife or child? Do they have any hepatitis B or C? 
I don’t think so 
Any medications at home currently? 
No.
Examination Findings:
Patient is alert
Vital signs: temperature is 97.4°F, blood pressure 118/76 mm Hg, pulse 64 beats per minute, and respiration 15 breaths per minute
Height: 175 cm, weight: 60 kg
Head and neck: Yellow eyes, yellow skin, no lymphadenopathy
Extremities: bruises on hands, leg edema
Abdominal examination: Distended abdomen with dullness to percussion in the lower area (ascites), Palpable spleen (2.5 cm below the costal margin), no palpable liver
Normal other exams
Laboratory & Imaging findings:
Total bilirubin: 65.2 µmol/L, Direct bilirubin 32.5 µmol/L, Albumin: 28 g/L
AST/ALT: 78/64 IU/L, INR: 1.78
HBV DNA: 2490 IU/mL, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe positive
Anti-HCV negative
HIV negative
Platelets: 98 x 10^9/L
AFP: 12.8 ng/mL, Creatinine: 77 µmol/L
Ultrasound: Fibrosis, splenomegaly, ascites, no nodules in the liver
Gastroscopy: Grade 3 esophageal varices

case_scenario:
Patient Background: 
Pham is a 31-year-old schoolteacher, currently 24 weeks pregnant with her second child. They do not know the child’s gender. Pham was diagnosed with hepatitis B several years ago, but has not received any treatment and wants to make sure she is okay at this stage in her pregnancy. She has tested HBsAg+ for several years. Her husband is 35 years old and works in a nearby office for a transportation and tourism service. They have a 2-year old girl Hue currently who is healthy and developing normally. Pham has not had any apparent liver symptoms or complications from her condition, and her pregnancy is otherwise normal. This is her second pregnancy. Her husband and daughter have never been tested for hepatitis.
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness
This is her second pregnancy, and the first child never got tested for Hepatitis B. Pham has no underlying diseases and is not taking any medications. Pham does not experience fatigue, loss of appetite, yellow eyes, or any bleeding. No liver enzymes and virus count tests were done, so no medications were taken. HIV and syphilis tests are negative.

Medical & Social History
No one has cirrhosis or HCC in Pham’s family. Her husband has never been tested with Hepatitis B or other liver-related illnesses. 
Opening statement: Doctor, I was diagnosed with hepatitis B a few years ago, and now that I am pregnant with my second child, I wanted to come here and check to make sure everything is okay.
Provider Questions with SP Responses:
When did you find out about hepatitis B? 
A few years ago.
How do you know about your hepatitis B status?
My HBsAg was positive a few years ago
Besides HBsAg, are there other laboratory tests, such as liver enzymes or virus count, that you took before?
I don’t think so
Have you received any antiviral treatment? 
No.

case_name_viet: viêm gan B mãn tính không xơ hóa
case_scenario:
Patient Background: 
Tran is a 44-year-old store manager who was diagnosed with chronic hepatitis B 29 years ago. He’s asymptomatic and attends biannual check-ups. He was diagnosed at age 15. He lives with his wife, who is 39, and 2 of their 3 sons in a three-bedroom apartment. They recently moved to this area when he got a promotion and his two children who live with him have started at a local school. His oldest has just gone to university. 
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness
Tran is currently very healthy and has never experienced any significant liver-related symptoms. He has been diagnosed with chronic Hepatitis B infection for 29 years and has never been on antiviral treatment. He just transferred to your facility because of the family relocation, and he visited for the first time for a regular check-up for his hepatitis B, which is scheduled to take place every 6 months. Family history: mother had hepatitis B.

Medical & Social History
He did not use injection drugs, smoked, and only drinks alcohol 1-2 times/month with 2-3 glasses of beer each time. He was previously tested negative for HCV and HIV. His mother was diagnosed with Hepatitis B. His siblings have never been tested with Hepatitis B. He was already married and had 3 children, and they had never been tested for Hepatitis B. 
Opening statement: Doctor, I have had chronic hepatitis B for many years and I recently moved to this area, so I am going to be seeing you from now on. It has been about 6 months since my last exam.

29 years ago.

I was diagnosed when I was 15 years old

No, because my previous doctor always said that I am not eligible for antiviral medication

No

No

No

case_name_eng: chronic hepatitis C with advanced fibrosis (F3), without cirrhosis and without co-infections

case_scenario:
Patient Background: 
Ngo is a 28-year-old auto mechanic who discovered he was anti-HCV positive after a blood donation he made two years ago. He never followed up until recently, because he began experiencing fatigue, joint pain, and nausea for the past 3 weeks. He is unmarried and lives in a small house by himself. He has lived here since he finished school at 19 years old. He had a blood transfusion after a traffic accident when he was 15. 
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness
He experienced a notable loss of appetite and nausea for the past 3 weeks, which has made it hard to do his job. Besides that, he also often feels pain in his knee joints and weakness in his legs. However, he had no fever, no shortness of breath, and no jaundice or yellow eyes. As he felt uncomfortable, he sought traditional medicine, but his condition did not improve. Then he went to the clinic, and previous tests revealed that liver enzymes were also elevated, so he went to your facility. 

Medical & Social History
He received a blood transfusion of red blood cells because of his accident 15 years ago. He hasn’t been vaccinated against Hepatitis B, hasn’t taken any drugs and does not drink alcohol, and does not smoke. Family medical history: no HCC and Hepatitis B, and C.
Opening statement: Doctor, I recently have felt very tired. My knees hurt, and I have had nausea. I went to a small clinic before coming here where they did some tests but my problems have not been resolved. 
Provider Questions with SP Responses:
How long have you been experiencing these symptoms? 
For the past 3 weeks.
What symptoms have you noticed? 
Loss of appetite, nausea, joint pain (particularly knee joints), fatigue, weakness in my leg
Do you have any fever or shortness of breath? 
No
Do your skin or eyes become yellow?
No

It is normal
Do you have leg swelling?
No
Do you have a fever?
No
Do you have any history of chronic illnesses or medications?
No
Any history of blood transfusions or surgeries? 
Yes, a transfusion after an accident 13 years ago
Have you used traditional or herbal medicine for your current symptoms? 
Yes, but it didn’t improve.
Have your family members been tested with Hepatitis? 
No.
Do you have any past history of liver disease? 
I got my anti-HCV test positive during blood donation 2 years ago
Did you get antiviral treatment?
No
For your current condition, have you taken any previous laboratory tests? 
Yes, from the clinic before I came here, the liver enzyme test results were elevated
Have you been vaccinated against Hepatitis B?
No
Do you have any family members who have liver disease?
No
Do you drink alcohol or smoke?
No
Do you know your hepatitis B status?
No
Do you know your HIV status?
No
Do you have a history of using unsafe injections?
No

case_name_eng: chronic viremic hepatitis C infection, co-infected with hepatitis B and HIV, compensated liver cirrhosis
case_name_viet: nhiễm trùng viêm gan C mạn tính, đồng nhiễm viêm gan B và HIV, xơ gan còn bù
case_scenario:
Patient Background: 
Dang is a 30-year-old living with HIV, and has been on antiretroviral therapy (ARV) since turning 21. He tested positive for HBsAg and anti-HCV at the time of HIV diagnosis but was never treated for hepatitis C. He works on a farm in the province, and is married to his wife Huong (30) for the last 6 years, and they have 3 young children, aged 2, 3, and 5. He now presents with jaundice and fatigue. He was an IV drug user who stopped 7 years ago, right before getting married, a little after he was diagnosed with HIV and started on ARV. He has no family history of liver disease. He is currently on TLD ARV regimen, with good antiretroviral therapy adherence. He has had an undetectable viral load and his wife and children have never been diagnosed with HIV.
Chief Complaint & History of Present Illness
He is a regular patient in your health facility. He has had yellowing eyes for the last 2 months, has also been tired almost all day. He has no fever, but often has nausea and sometimes vomiting. He has normal bowel movements and decreased appetite. There was no family history of liver cancer or Hepatitis B or C among his mothers, siblings, wife, and children.

Medical & Social History
He was diagnosed with HIV in November 2017 and is on ARV treatment, a TDF/3TC/EFV regimen. When he was enrolled, he was tested and got anti-HCV (+) and HBsAg (+). At that time, he was not offered an HCV RNA test or antiviral treatment. He has a history of using IV drugs when he was young, but has stopped for the past 7 years.
Opening statement: Doctor, I feel tired all the time, and my eyes have been a bit yellow since this happened. I am on antiretroviral therapy, is this related? 
Provider Questions with SP Responses:
Have you had any symptoms recently? 
Yes, yellow eyes, nausea, vomiting, and tiredness.

2 months

No

No

No

I think it is as usual

It decreased

No

No
Since when have you been taking ARV treatment?
Since I turned 21.
What regimen that you take? 
TDF/3TC/EFV
Are you taking your HIV treatment regularly? 
Yes, daily.
Do you have a history of liver disease?
When I enrolled in ARV treatment, I was tested and got anti-HCV (+) and HBsAg (+)
Besides that, did you take other laboratory tests such as an HCV RNA test?
No
Did you take any antiviral or hepatitis medication?
No
Do you drink alcohol? 
No.
Do you have a history of using injection drugs?
Yes, when I was young. But I have stopped for 7 years, since I got married.
Do you have any family members with a history of liver cancer or Hepatitis B, or C?
No
Have your family members been tested for hepatitis? 
No.
